coredata.dc:title: Fe-N coordination moieties regulate the defect formation in carbon nanomaterial for efficient peroxydisulfate activation: Significant role of surface complex
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xuefeng
Zhu, Jinyuan
Hayat, Waseem
Zhang, Yongqing
Huang, Shaobin
Jiang, Ran
coredata.dc:description: An iron (Fe)-modified carbon dots (CDs) doped ZIF-8 zeolite imidazole metal-organic frame pyrolyzed composite (5Fe@NC) was prepared and then evaluated as a peroxydisulfate (PDS) activator for the oxidative removal of organic pollutants. The 5Fe@NC/PDS system not only has a good recovery performance, wide pH adaptability, and low activation energy, but also is resistant to environmental interference. The quenching experiments, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) detection, and electrochemical analysis all revealed that unlike the widely reported singlet oxygen (1O2) dominated nonradical mechanism, an electron transfer process (ETP) involving a surface bound PDS complex played the main role in pollutants’ degradation by 5Fe@NC/PDS system. The FeNx, defect, graphitic N and hydroxyl group (-OH) acting as active sites, prompted the 5Fe@NC-PDS complex to capture electrons (e−) from acetaminophen (ACT). This study provides a new approach for xFe@NCs rational design, and deepen the understanding of FeNx activation mechanism of PDS in carbon nanomaterials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An iron (Fe)-modified carbon dots (CDs) doped ZIF-8 zeolite imidazole metal-organic frame pyrolyzed composite (5Fe@NC) was prepared and then evaluated as a peroxydisulfate (PDS) activator for the oxidative removal of organic pollutants. The 5Fe@NC/PDS system not only has a good recovery performance, wide pH adaptability, and low activation energy, but also is resistant to environmental interference. The quenching experiments, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) detection, and electrochemical analysis all revealed that unlike the widely reported singlet oxygen (1O2) dominated nonradical mechanism, an electron transfer process (ETP) involving a surface bound PDS complex played the main role in pollutants’ degradation by 5Fe@NC/PDS system. The FeNx, defect, graphitic N and hydroxyl group (-OH) acting as active sites, prompted the 5Fe@NC-PDS complex to capture electrons (e−) from acetaminophen (ACT). This study provides a new approach for xFe@NCs rational design, and deepen the understanding of FeNx activation mechanism of PDS in carbon nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced oxidation processes based on persulfate（PS-AOPs) has attained a worldwide attention due to their lower redox potential and more capability of efficient degradation of organic pollutants than those of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [1,2]. The traditional peroxydisulfate (PDS)-AOPs are mainly based on the non-selective oxidation of pollutants through hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and sulfate radicals (SO4 •−), which are originated from the homolytic peroxide (O-O) bond cleavage in PDS [1,3]. Compared with the conventional degradation through radical pathway, the oxidation capacities of the nonradical degradation systems are moderate. The extensively reported major PDS processes employed on the basis of nonradical pathways include the direct electron-transfer processes (ETP), PDS-complex, singlet oxygenation (1O2), and the high-valent metals [4]. The surface structure of carbon (C) material regulation is the important way to improve catalytic performance during nonradical system, such as fabricating structural defects [5], regulating pore structures [6], and altering surface functional groups [7,8]. Heteroatom doping is an easy and efficient approach to realize it. Iron (Fe) species as an environmental-friendly, abundant metal and have the ability to coordinate with other species (such as nitrogen (N) [9] or sulfur (S) [10]) to modify the surface structure of the C material, which increases the potential for excellent PDS activation; however, the leaching of Fe ions limits the practical application. Therefore, to synthesize a Fe complex that can modify the surface structure of material and inhibit Fe leaching is of great significance for the rational design of emerging activators for advanced oxidation processes.

Carbon Dots (CDs) is a type of environmentally-friendly nanocrystal, primarily composed of C, hydrogen (H), oxygen (O) and N, with an approximately spherical shape and direct penetration less than 10 nm [11,12]. Many e−-donating groups (-OH, amino (-NH2)) and e−-withdrawing groups (carbonyl (-CO) and carboxyl (-COOH)) on the CDs surface can be easily modified to form strong complexes with metal ions [12]. Lin et al. [13] synthesized NH2-CQDs and observed that complexation between ruthenium (Ru) cations and -NH2 was much stronger than that between the Ru cations and other N containing groups. Liu et al. [14] observed that the CDs surface was enriched with -OH, which can be complexed with ferric (Fe3+) ions, leading to CDs aggregation. Therefore, Fe leaching can be significantly improved by the polymerization of CDs and Fe ions.

Carbon materials, such as graphene oxide (r-GO), carbon nanotubes (CNT), nanodiamond (ND), and mesoporous carbons (MCs) have been used to activate PDS [7,15,16]. However, the catalytic properties of raw C are always unsatisfactory. Recently, novel hybrid catalysts of metal-nitrogen co-doped carbon materials (M-N-C) has got attention as the ideal candidates to replace the traditional metal activators of PDS due to their higher superiority and stability [17]. Similarly, various strategies have been successfully applied to improve the catalytic activity of N and Fe co-doping in many fields [18,19]. N doping can control C lattice spin density, charge distribution, and the generation of perceptible bonding disturbances [20,21]. Especially, the graphitic N would take e− from adjacent C atoms, which will subsequently encourage the adjacent C atoms to take e− from the PDS oxygen (-O−) [22]. Fe could coordinate with N (FeNx, x denotes the coordination number) regulating not only the electronic structures [23] but also inducing the spinning [24], which promotes the C frame lattice distortion resulting in the subsequent formation of a large number of defect sites on surface. Meanwhile, the surface defects provide coordinatively unsaturated C sites to promote the ETP [25,26]. However, Cheng et al. [5] discovered that excessive defects cause the partial C transformation from sp2 to sp3, which impairs the conjugated π system and deteriorates the ETP. It has been reported that the defect structure of pine needle-derived biochar pyrolyzed at high temperature activated peroxymonosulfate (PMS) to produce •OH and SO4 •− for the degradation of 1,4-dioxan [15]. Therefore, the defect generation as well as the relationship between defects and the active substances needs further investigation.

Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF-8) can be synthesized from the thermal treatment of inorganic metal nodes and organic bridging linkers, having a regular 12-hedral 3D network structure and favorable porous architecture [9,27]. The unique microporous structure of ZIF-8 and inherent coordination of M-N species result in synthesis of M-Nx structures [23]. Metal ions (Fe3+, Co3+, Ni3+, Cu2+) and intermetallic compounds (Co3O4, Fe2O3) have been anchored on ZIF-8 carbon skeletons to activate PDS/PMS for organics degradation [28–30]. Zinc (Zn) species can be evaporated at high-temperature, which provides the opportunity for FeNx synthesis while forming evenly distributed pores [9]. Further, heteroatoms with different atomic radii and electronegativities can modulate the inherently electroactive sites thereby enhancing the defective sites [16,31]. Some researchers have used ZIF-8 to trap Fe salt molecules (such as FeSO4, FeCl3, Fe(NO3)3) to form FeNx species [23,32] emphasizing on the investigation of the role of FeNx in catalytic process. However, the catalysts obtained by this approach suffer from an insufficient density of FeNX sites. Few studies have reported that direct introduction of FeNx species as precursors might overcome this issue. And, it still has hindered the in-depth understanding of the potential catalytic mechanism as well as the practical applications of FeNx.

Herein, we prepared xFe@NC by adding different proportions of Fe-CDs to ZIF-8 through high temperature annealing along with the analysis of morphology, structure, and physicochemical properties of the xFe@NCs. Then, the catalytic performance of xFe@NC towards PDS activation was systematically evaluated by using acetaminophen (ACT) as the target pollutant to be degraded oxidatively. The mechanism of PDS activation with 5Fe@NC was thoroughly studied using reaction kinetics, radicals quenching experiments, EPR detection, and electrochemical analysis. This study deepens the understanding of the generation of surface defects and PDS-AOPs mechanism during the employment of FeNx to regulate C-based activators. In addition, it also provides the technical support for the C nanomaterial mediated PDS activation in practical applications.

All chemicals were used without any additional purification. Dicyandiamide (DCDA, C2H4N4, 99%), citric acid (CA, C6H8O7, 98%), ammonium ferric citrate (AFC, C6H8FeNO7, AR), 2-methylimidazole (2-MeIm, C5H8N2, 98%), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2.6 H2O, 98%), sodium persulfate (PDS, Na2S2O8, 99%), superoxide dismutase (SOD, 20000 u/mg), 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMP, 99%) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, 97%), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 98%), methyl alcohol (MeOH, CH4O, 99%), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA, C4H10O, 98.5%), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ, C6H4O2, 98%), benzoic acid (BA, C7H6O2, 98%), phenol (PE, C6H6O, 99%), deuterium oxide (D2O, 99%), were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemistry Inc., China. Acetaminophen (ACT, C8H9NO2, 98%), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP, C6H5OCl, 98%), furfuryl alcohol (FFA, C5H10O2, 98%), were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai China). Sodium perchlorate (NaClO4, 99%) was purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water (DI) was provided by a Milli-Q System.

Fe-CDs were hydrothermally synthesized using AFC mixed with DCDA as precursors. The detailed process employed for the synthesis of Fe-CDs is as follows. 15.0 mmol AFC and 15.0 mmol DCDA were dissolved in 60 mL DI. Then, the mixture was sonicated for 30 min to achieve complete dissolution. This solution was hydrothermally reacted at 220 ℃ for 12 h. Then, the solution was passed through a 0.22 µm water filtration membrane to obtain Fe-CDs liquid.

The xFe-CDs@ZIF-8 s (x = 1, 3, 5, 7, 10) were synthesized and optimized by using a reported method [33]. Briefly, 4.12 mmol (1.225 g) Zn(NO3)2.6 H2O and 32.96 mmol (2.761 g) 2-MeIm were separately dissolved in 40 mL MeOH to obtain two solutions A (Zn(NO3)2.6 H2O) and B (2-MeIM). Then, different volumes (0, 0.5, 1.5, 2.5, 3.5, 5.0 mL) of Fe-CDs solution add to solution A and stirred vigorously for 30 min. Then, solution B was slowly added to a vigorously stirred above solution, and keep stirring for 4 h. The mixture was left to stand for 24 h, washed 3 times with MeOH and then dried at 50 °C.

The xFe-CDs@ZIF-8 (x = 1, 3, 5, 7, 10) powders were placed into a crucible and annealed at 900 ℃ for 2 h (heating rate to 900 ℃ of 5 ℃ min−1) under N2 protection to get xFe@NC (x = 1, 3, 5, 7, 10), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization, catalytic activity tests, and analytical methods
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization techniques of catalysts are listed in Text S1. The procedures of catalytic activity tests, analytical methods of organics, Fe ions and ROS are list in Text S2 and Text S3, respectively.

The entire synthetic process of xFe@NC (x = 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10) is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1a. The SEM images of the obtained NC, NCDsC and 5Fe@NC samples ( Fig. 2a-2f) show a regular polyhedral structure, proving the successful synthesis of the ZIF-8 composite. Compared with the compact morphology and smooth surface of NC, NCDsC possesses a structure with smaller molecular diameter, and the porous spots on surface in the presence of CDs. Furthermore, the introduction of Fe-CDs changed the molecular structure of surface, which was comprised of the coordination of FeNx that promoted the mutual fusion of ZIF-8 composite materials and subsequent formation of a large number of pores. The size of any individual ZIF-8 molecule is only about 40 nm in diameter, which is about one-tenth the size of ZIF-8 composites in NC. In addition, no highly crystalline Fe species appears in the TEM images (Fig. 2g). Meanwhile, SEM images show the porous C in 5Fe@NC lacking any identifiable Fe nanoparticles (NPs) (Fig. 2h). This may be attributed to the fine spatial constraint strategy of ZIF-8, which effectively prevented the agglomeration of unstable Fe nanoparticles (NPs) during pyrolysis [9]. The elemental mapping of 5Fe@NC shows the even distribution of C, N, O and Fe in the NC structure (Fig. 2i). C is the main element and Fe content is 1.15% (Fig. S1). Theoretically, Fe content is 0.83%; while, ICP-OES detection of the sample (processing method in Text S5) shows that Fe content is 0.274% (Table S3). These findings indicate that the Fe species were successfully assembled into the ZIF-8.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis shows that CDs have a characteristic peak at 24.5°. Fe-CDs having a characteristic peak at 26.7° (Fig. S2a) indicates that the presence of Fe3+ affected the interlayer spacing of the C layers of C [34]. Meanwhile, NC, NCDsC and xFe@NC exhibit two broad peaks corresponding to C planes in graphene (25.3° to (002), 43.7° to (101)) ( Fig. 3a). Additionally, no characteristic peaks of Fe or its oxides are found, which may be due to the presence of fine particle of Fe beyond detection limit and the high dispersion of the Fe atoms in the skeleton [9].

The local structural properties of different carbonized samples were further analyzed by Raman spectra. As shown in Fig. 3b, two characteristic peaks attributed to the D and G bands appear at approximately 1350 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. The relative strength ratio, ID/IG, can be used to reflect the degree of defects in C materials. N doping is conducive to the formation of defect sites [21]. The respective ID/IG of NC, NCDsC and 5Fe@NC are 0.94, 1.05 and 1.08 indicating that more defect sites were formed after Fe-CDs doping. This phenomenon corresponds to the EPR findings (Fig. 3c). EPR results show that compared with CDs doping, the introduction of Fe-CDs increased the surface defects (g = 2.003) [35]. This result may be attributed to the coordination of Fe and N; where, the FeNx would snatch e− from C, then form the C+ and defect structures. Further, the ID/IG increased from 0.98 to 1.16 with the increase of Fe-CDs contents.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for the catalysts under different degrees of decompression show the distribution of pore size and the specific surface area of catalysts (Fig. S4 and Table S2). Compared with NC, the number of mesoporous NCDsC and xFe@NC significantly increased providing sufficient active sites for the subsequent catalytic reactions. Although, the BET of 5Fe@NC was not as large as that of NCDsC; however, its number of mesopores was higher than that of NCDsC.

XPS characterization was conducted to analyze the composition and chemical state of NC, NCDsC and 5Fe@NC (Fig. S5). There are three main peaks at 284, 398 and 532 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s respectively. The high-resolution XPS N 1 s spectra of the different catalysts were collected to quantify the type and content of N dopants. Five peaks with binding energies at 398.2, 399.2, 400.2, 401.1 and 402.5 eV were fitted, which were respectively assigned to pyridinic N, FeNx, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and N-oxides [33] (Table. S5). There were only three N species, pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and N-oxides found in NC and NCDsC (Fig. 3d). In addition, pyridinic N content in NCDsC was higher than that in NC. However, there are five N species (pyridinic N, FeNx, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and N-oxides) in 5Fe@NC (Fig. 3d). Meanwhile, the formation of FeNx and graphitic N may be due to the action of Fe-CDs, which changes the formation of N species in the ZIF-8 composites polymerization.

C1s spectra of the three samples can be split into CC (sp2-C, 284.7 ∼284.8 eV), C-C (sp3-C, 286.0 ∼286.3 eV) and O-C-OH (288.2 ∼288.9 eV) (Fig. S6) [6]. The O1s spectra can be divided into 532.14 ∼532.38 eV and 534.28 ∼534.37 eV corresponding to C-OH/C-O-C and CO respectively (Fig. 3e) [36]. Table S6 shows the percentages of C-OH/C-O-C and CO in the fitted peak area. The data show that the proportion of C-OH/C-O-C in 5Fe@NC is higher than that of NC and NCDsC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of pollutants (PCs) on different catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Surface characteristics of the catalyst greatly affect its adsorption properties. Hence, ACT was used as the target organic pollutant to determine the adsorption properties of materials ( Fig. 4a). The results show that 5Fe@NC had the largest adsorption towards ACT reaching 18.8% at 30 min. The SBET of NCDsC was higher than that of 5Fe@NC; however, the adsorption capacity of 5Fe@NC was larger due to the presence of the many mesoporous pores, resulting in from the incorporation of Fe. Many mesoporous pores were still present in xFe@NC. The comparison of NC and NCDsC shows that the introduction of N changed the materials’ SBET. However, it had no effect on pore size, and it showed the negligible effects on ACT adsorption. In order to evaluate the adsorption of pollutants with different molecular diameters by 5Fe@NC, PE (0.62 nm [37]), 4-CP (about 0.8 nm [38]) and ACT (0.894 nm [39]) were selected as the target pollutants. The adsorption extents were found to be 6.9%, 15.5% and 18.8%, respectively (Fig. S8). Thus, the larger molecular diameters resulted in more adsorption due to the existence of many mesoporous surfaces (Fig. 4d). Previous studies have shown that surface functional groups, and surface charge distributions have certain effects on adsorption [40,41]. Fe atoms can also act as the adsorption sites [24] and coordinate with N in ACT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The PDS activation performance of catalysts
Oxidation kinetics of PCs on different catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the activation potential of different catalysts for PDS activation, oxidative experiments were conducted using ACT as the target pollutant; while, its degradation kinetics was compared using NC, NCDsC and xFe@NC as PDS activators. As illustrated in Fig. S9, 5Fe@NC/PDS system shows the highest pseudo-first-order kinetics (k = 0.1569 min−1) for ACT by completely degrading it (100% removal rate) within 20 min. Meanwhile, almost no ACT was removed within 30 min in the separate PDS system (no 5Fe@NC) (Fig. 4c). To determine the optimized FeNx content in NC, the degradation performance of 1Fe@NC, 3Fe@NC, 5Fe@NC, 7Fe@NC,10Fe@NC were compared. 5Fe@NC has the highest reactivity among all the activators followed by 3Fe@NC and then by 7Fe@NC (Fig. 4b). The lower reactivity of 1Fe@NC and 3Fe@NC is likely due to their lower Fe concentration. On the other side, the 7Fe@NC and 10Fe@NC might be negatively influenced by the large amount of FeNx and the formation of excessive surface defects. Excessive defects impair the conjugated π system of partial C and deteriorate the ETP [5]. These defects change the activity of neighboring C atoms, the ID/IG as well as the activation rate (Fig. 4f). By comparing the NC/PDS/ACT and NCDsC /PDS/ACT systems, it can be found that the introduction of N improves the catalytic reaction rate. Previous research [42] also supports these findings that the defective edges may be increased due to the doping of C materials with N as evident from the SEM analysis (Fig. 2a-b; 2d-e). The reaction rate was positively correlated with the surface area of mesopores (Smes) and the pore size (Fig. 4e). Normally, the leaching of metal ions in metallic catalysts is unavoidable. The Fe leaching in current study was lower than 0.1 mg/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of operational conditions on ACT degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Since 5Fe@NC has the highest activation potential among the tested activators, a series of the experiments were conducted to determine the impact of various parameters on degradation by the 5Fe@NC/PDS system.

The activator dosage had a significant impact on ACT degradation ( Fig. 5a). As the activator dosage increased from 0.02 g·L−1 to 0.1 g·L−1, the ACT degradation rate remarkably increased from 0.074 min−1 to 0.511 min−1 (Fig. S11a). In addition, ACT degradation under different activator dosages fit well with the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Fig. 5b). The increased degradation with increasing activator dosage can be attributed to the fact that more activator results in the elevated availability of active sites. This increased availability facilitates PDS activation on 5Fe@NC surfaces.

The oxidant concentration is important in PDS-based degradation. Therefore, the impact of PDS concentration (0.5–6.0 mmol/L) on ACT degradation was evaluated (Fig. 5d). Normally, higher PDS concentration at the same catalytic dosage results in a faster degradation reaction rate [43]. In the 5Fe@NC/PDS system, as the PDS concentration increased from 0.5 mmol/L to 2.0 mmol/L, ACT degradation increased, along with the corresponding k value from 0.0682 min−1 to 0.1569 min−1 (Fig. S11b). However, a PDS concentration over 2.0 mmol/L had negligible influence on ACT degradation by 5Fe@NC/PDS system, which indicates that the 5Fe@NC/PDS system may not be a radical-based oxidation system. Therefore, it is investigated in detail in Section 3.4.

Further, to evaluate the activation performance of 5Fe@NC, experiments were conducted using different initial ACT concentrations. The results show that raising the initial ACT concentration from 5.0 mg/L to 60.0 mg/L, the after 20 min degradation efficiency dropped from 100% to 73.3% (Fig. 5e). However, it is significant that the reaction rates at 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0 and 60.0 mg/L were 0.1735, 0.1569, 0.1742, 0.1088 and 0.0927 min−1, respectively (Fig. S11c). The pseudo-first-order kinetics was almost unchanged when the initial concentration was less than 20.0 mg/L, which indicates that a high reaction rate can be achieved under low ACT concentration. On increasing the ACT concentration to 40.0 mg/L, the reaction rate decrease may result from insufficient active sites provided by the 5Fe@NC.

The solution pH is a significant factor affecting ROS’s generation in chemical oxidation processes [44]. For instance, Fenton and Fenton-like processes are also susceptible to pH variations, hindering their practical application [1]. Therefore, effect of initial pH on ACT degradation in the 5Fe@NC/PDS system was studied. As illustrated in Fig. 5f, ACT is effectively removed over a wide pH range (3−9). This might result in from the stable generation of ROS in the 5Fe@NC/PDS system at all initial pHs. The isoelectric point (pHpzc) analysis showed the pHpzc of the 5Fe@NC was 1.41 (Fig. S7) and the pKa of ACT is 9.38 [45], which means that Zeta potential of 5Fe@NC would decrease with the pH increase. When pH was below 1.41, the charge on 5Fe@NC surface was positive and the peroxydisulfate (S2O8 2−) ion was electrostatically attracted to 5Fe@NC surface. The negative charge on the surface of the 5Fe@NC at pH greater than 9.38 would result in deprotonation of ACT, which is not conducive to the adsorption of ACT and PDS. Therefore, an initial pH in the range of 1.41–9.38 is expected to have no significant effect on ACT degradation, showing good potential for 5Fe@NC application.

Since PDS can be activated to oxidize organic pollutants through radical and non-radical pathways in metal-involved catalytic systems, it was necessary to determine the main ROS in the 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT system. Quenching experiments was used to determine the ROS involved in the degradation reactions. It was observed that •OH and SO4 •− were the representative radicals having high reduction potentials (2.5 −3.1 VNHE for SO4 •−/SO4 2− and 1.8 −2.7 VNHE for •OH/OH) to deoxidize the pollutants [46,47]. Previous studies proved that MeOH and TBA can be used to identify the radical pathway, since MeOH containing α-H is a potent scavenger for •OH (k = 1.2–2.8 × 108 M−1s−1) and SO4 •− (k = 1.6–7.8 × 107 M−1s−1) [48,49]; while, TBA without α-H have higher rate constant of •OH (k = 3.8–7.6 × 108 M−1s−1) than SO4 •− (k = 4.0–9.1 × 105 M−1s−1) [50]. The quenching results show that adding excessive MeOH (molar ratio MeOH:PDS, 500:1), barely changed the after 30 min removal efficiencies ( Fig. 6a), which indicates that there is almost no involvement of •OH and SO4 •− in ACT degradation by 5Fe@NC/PDS system. However, when MeOH and TBA were added at a 1000:1 molar ratio, the removal efficiencies were reduced by 9.8% and 31.9%. These findings are different from a previous study that reported the reaction between ACT and ROS occurred on the catalyst surface [51]. MeOH and TBA can be adsorbed by the catalyst before ACT due to their hydrophobicity [52], and TBA has a longer C chain than MeOH, making it easier to be collected on catalyst surface and thus inhibited the reaction between ROS and ACT. Upon the separate addition of MeOH or TBA, the adsorption of pollutants on the catalyst was also inhibited (Fig. 6c). In addition, previous research has shown that TBA is unsuitable for free radical identification in C-involved systems because it prevents e− transfer on the C surface [21,52]. PE not only can be used to confirm the presence of •OH, SO4 •−and 1O2 in the reaction system, but can verify the surface reaction of the system, since it inhibits the direct contact between ACT and the catalyst [53]. Keeping the concentration of PE at 20.0 and 200.0 mmol/L (molar ratio PE:PDS, 10:1 and 100:1), the removal rates of ACT were 86.1% and 78.6% (Fig. S12). This indicates that the direct reaction on the surface played an important role in the 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT system.

Furthermore, BQ, a superoxide radical (O2 •−) scavenger (k = 0.9–1.0 ×109 M−1s−1) [40,54], accelerated the reaction rate instead of inhibiting it (Fig. 6d). BQ can be oxidized by e− transfer, thus influencing ACT removal [55]. Superoxide dismutase (SOD) only reacts with O2 •−, producing O2 and H2O2 [56]. The addition of 20.0 mg/L SOD resulted in no inhibition of the reaction, which excludes the involvement of O2 •− in the 5Fe@NC/PDS system.

Since the 5Fe@NC/PDS system showed great degradation potential after the suppression of free radicals, the nonradical pathways such as the generation and function of 1O2 were investigated. Due to its high reaction rate, FFA was used to scavenge 1O2 (k = 1.2 × 108 M−1 S−1) [5]. The presence of 20.0 mmol/L FFA slightly inhibited ACT degradation and the rate constant (k) decreased from 0.1569 min−1 to 0.0918 min−1. However, a subsequent experiment contradicted this result. D2O can extend the lifetime of 1O2 (30–32 μs in D2O, 2.0 μs in H2O) [4]. Using D2O in the reaction system slightly inhibited the degradation compared with using H2O (Fig. 6f), which indicates that no 1O2 was present in the 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT system. To more clearly determine the existence of 1O2, FFA concentration was detected both with and without PDS. The analysis showed that the FFA concentration remained unchanged during the reaction (Fig. 6e), which indicates that no 1O2 was present, to consume the FFA.

EPR is a common method for detecting ROS. DMPO, a common spin trapping agent reacts with •OH and SO4 •− to form adducts [57]. A small amount of •OH, generated by the self-decomposition of PDS in the system was found; while, no SO4 •− was found ( Fig. 7a). The DMPOX formation may be due to PDS-complex taking an e− from DMPO. TEMP reacts with 1O2 to form a 1:1:1 signal peak. The results show that the 1O2 peak did not change after the addition of 5Fe@NC (Fig. 7b). Thus, EPR confirms there were almost no ROS formed in the reaction system.

During the e− transfer processes, the introduction of organics leads to a significant increase in PDS consumption [4,55] (Fig. 7c). K2Cr2O7 acts as a common quencher for e− transfer [4]. The removal rates of ACT decreased from 0.1569 min−1 to 0.0445 min−1 in the presence of K2Cr2O7 (10.0 mmol/L) (Fig. S16b), implying that e− transfer acted as main process in 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT system and that ACT degradation by K2Cr2O7 needs an activator. Pre-mixing the catalysts and PDS can significantly affect the degradation rate of organics in radical dominated reactions [58]. In 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT system, the reaction rate changed from 0.1569 min−1 with no premixing to 0.1538 min−1 after premixing for 5 min (Fig. S17). However, there was no significant difference in the reaction rates after premixing for 5 and 10 min (Fig. 7d), which confirmed that ACT degradation was a non-radical dominated reaction. The electrochemical tests (cyclic voltammetry (CV)，linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), and amperometric i-t curve (I-t)) can detect the presence of ETP in the system. The CV results show that compared with 5Fe@NC, the peak shifts downward in 5Fe@NC/PDS (Fig. 7e), which proves that 5Fe@NC-PDS complex was formed. With the addition of ACT, a pair of redox peaks appeared, which proves that 5Fe@NC-PDS complex captures the e− of ACT. Furthermore, the LSV results are similar to the CV results (Fig. 7f). The I-t curve (Fig. S14b) shows that the current density changed significantly upon the addition of PDS at 150 s and ACT at 360 s. A galvanic oxidation process (GOP) to separate BPA and PMS into two half cells was designed by Huang et al. [59] to prove the mechanism more directly. GOP (the experimental procedure is presented in Text S6) results show that a background current is found in the absence of ACT (Fig. S15); and after adding ACT, the current in the system increased significantly, indicating an e− transfer process. Therefore, the experimental results prove that the PDS-complex was the dominant ROS in the 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT system.

To detect the reactions within the system, the IR spectra of 5Fe@NC, PDS, 5Fe@NC/PDS and 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT were taken. Compared to PDS alone，the S-O band shifted from 1131 to 1108 cm−1 when 5Fe@NC was added to the PDS solution ( Fig. 8a). This is mainly due to the interaction between 5Fe@NC and PDS, which reduced e− density of S-O bond in generated PDS complex [55,60]. The S-O band is not shifted in 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT system. Hence, the 5Fe@NC-PDS complex was the active species, which was produced through the catalyst interact with PDS on surface, and affected by the characteristics of pollutants [61].

To confirm the types of a surface reaction, fluoride ion (F−) was used to detect the existence of -OH on catalyst surface. -OH can form hydrogen bonds that inhibit the combination of PDS and activator [62]. The degradation of ACT was 98%, 89% and 56% in 20 min at F- concentration of 10.0, 25.0 and 50.0 mmol/L, respectively. The GOP experiment also shows that the presence of F- has an inhibitory effect on the increase of current intensity in the system (Fig. S15). Therefore, the surface -OH was the active site in system, and it played a role in promoting the ETP.

The introduction of the Fe species allows N coordination to form FeNx active sites, and regulates the e− transfer between adjacent C. To better evaluate the Fe’s contribution to catalytic reaction, a KSCN toxicity test was carried out [51,63]. As the concentration of KSCN increased, ACT degradation gradually decreased indicating that the Fe species played a major role in the reaction (Fig. 8c).

LC-MS was applied to probe the ACT degradation pathway in 5Fe@NC/PDS system (the method as listed in Text S8). The possible intermediates are listed in Table S7, while two possible degradation pathways are deduced ( Fig. 9). The first pathway (Ⅰ) manifested that phenoxyl radical (A) was generated. Then phenoxyl would be oxidized to quinones (B). Then, A and B would dimerize each other to form C. As reported previously that the bond dissociation energy (BDE) of BHT in water (81.4 kJ mol−1) is lower than that of ACT (125.0 kJ mol−1) [64] and PE (87.7 kJ mol−1). In addition, BHT is a chain-breaking antioxidant that eliminates the reactivity of phenoxyl radicals [57]. The value of the rate constant (k) decreased from 0.1569 min−1 to 0.0538 min−1 in the presence of 10.0 mmol/L BHT (Fig. S18b). Further, the phenomenon still appeared when PE was the target pollutant (Fig. S18c). According to the second pathway (Ⅱ) the acetyl group connected to the nitrogen would be displaced to generate D. Then, the further oxidation gradually led to the generation of p-nitrophenol (E), hydroquinone (F) and p-benzoquinone (G) respectively. The initial solution was colorless and then the solution was changed yellow during the reaction, indicating the generation of p-benzoquinone during the reaction. Finally, CO2 and H2O were evolved through continuous oxidation.

Structure defects of carbon-based materials can regulate the e− distribution on the catalyst and thus change the catalytic performance of the materials. The Raman spectrum shows ID/IG decreased from 1.08 (before reaction) to 1.03 (reaction 1 time) and 0.94 (reaction 10 times) (Fig. S3), indicating that the defect acted as the main active site in the reaction. To better understand the activation process, the XPS spectra before the degradation reaction, reaction 1 time and reaction 10 times were obtained. As shown in Fig. 8d and Table S5, the content of graphitic N decreased significantly from 15.7% to 11.1% after one reaction and to 10.1% after 10 reactions. Meanwhile, the FeNx content changed from 26.9% to 24.5% and 20.5% after 1 and 10 reactions. However, the proportion of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N decreased with the increase of reaction times. Graphitic N can adsorb the e− from neighboring C atoms to form an e−-deficient C, which is conducive to obtaining e− from the pollutants and promoting the ETP. From the O 1 s XPS results (Fig. 8e), it was found that the content of -OH decreased after the reaction indicates that -OH participated in the catalytic ACT degradation process and there was conversion of catalyst -OH to carbonyl group (-CO). In addition, -OH is an e−-donating group, which is conducive to PDS adsorption and -OH also serves as the active center of ETP [6]. These results show that the FeNx, defect, graphitic N and -OH were the main active sites during the activation process (Fig. 1b). The introduction of the Fe atoms coordinated with N atoms, increased surface defects of C nanomaterials, and improved the PDS activation efficiency.

In conclusion, an e−-transfer involving PDS-complex dominated mechanism in the 5Fe@NC/PDS system for ACT removal are illustrated as follows:

To further explore the influence of background species on the catalytic performance of 5Fe@NC/PDS system in practical application, numerous typical inorganic anions were used. NaClO4 can be used to analyze the effect of anion strength. There was a negligible effect with the NaClO4 concentration from 1.0 to 50.0 mmol/L (Fig. S19). Previous studies indicate that Cl− reacts with SO4 •− and •OH to produce fewer reactive Cl• radicals resulting in a significant inhibition of pollutant’s degradation [65]. Humic acid (HA) also competes for ROS thus inhibiting the pollutant degradation [21]. As shown in Fig. 10, the presence of Cl−, NO3 − and HA exhibited the negligible effects on degradation in the 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT system. These results clearly show that there were no free radicals in this system. However, the addition of HPO4 2−, HCO3 −and CO3 2− resulted in an obvious inhibition. The pH of 10.0 mmol/L HPO4 2−, 10.0 mmol/L HCO3 −, and 10.0 mmol/L CO3 2− were 8.1, 8.3 and 11.1, respectively. At pH buffered around 8, the ACT degradation dramatically decreased to 0.0649 min−1 (Fig. S20); previously, it was shown that ETP is greatly reduced in basic conditions (pH=11) [66]. This indicates that ACT degradation was obviously decreased may be for having connection with the pH of reaction solution; while, ETP is operative in our degradation system.

Evaluating the stability of materials is an important aspect of their practical use, so, continuous bath experiments for ACT degradation were carried out. As shown in Fig. S21a, 5Fe@NC has high stability; where, the degradation efficiency remained 88.3% and 70.7% after the 5th and 10th cycle, respectively. The reason for the decreased reaction rate may be that the intermediates generated during the degradation were adsorbed on the pores of catalyst surface, thus minimized the PDS access to active sites of activator. And, after the completion of reaction, the insufficient active sites resulted in reducing PDS activation and the lower removal of ACT in follow each cycle. The TOC removal in 5Fe@NC/PDS/ACT system was 38.12% at 120 min (Fig. S21b).

Five common organic contaminants (ACT, RhB, 4-CP, Phenol, BA) were used to assess the universality of 5Fe@NC. As illustrated in Fig. S22b, the degradation rate of all pollutants was high up to 30 min, after which, the degradation rate decreased significantly. The degradation rate was still relatively high for e−-poor pollutant BA, in contrast to the BA degradation inhibition caused by MeOH addition (Fig. S23). Due to different chemical structure of pollutants, degradation mechanism for different pollutants might change as shown by Huang et. al [67]. For a more comprehensive assessment of the practicality of 5Fe@NC, natural water (South China University of Technology), artificial lake water (South China University of Technology) and central lake water (Central Lake of University City, Guangzhou) were used for degradation experiments (Text S9). As shown in Fig. S22a, 5Fe@NC expressed excellent ACT degradation in natural water. But the degradation took 90 min in artificial lake water and central lake water, the main reason could be that the lake contains a lot of impurities, and the active sites and pores of 5Fe@NC were filled with impurities limiting the exposure of active sites.

This study reports the synthesis, and characterization of a Fe@NC composite containing dodecahedral structures with surface defects. It also describes the kinetics and applicability of the composite in PDS activation. The quenching experiments, EPR characterization, electrochemical measurements, and GOP indicate that the degradation of ACT is primarily due to the effect of PDS-complex. Benefitting from e− transfer mechanism, the 5Fe@NC/PDS system showed high resistance to weakly alkaline inorganic ions, and the system has excellent recycling properties. Thus, 5Fe@NC/PDS system provides a new concept for organic pollutant removal from water.
